title: Lasso Regression (L1)
concept: Lasso adds a penalty equal to the absolute size of weights, shrinking less important features to exactly zero.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In Ridge Regression (L2), how does the model prevent overfitting?

options.text: By adding the squared values of the weights as a penalty to the cost function
By setting less important weights to exactly zero
By removing random rows of data from training

exercises.explain: Right! Ridge Regression (L2) adds a squared weight penalty to shrink weights close to zero, but it never sets them to exactly zero.

cards.title: Packing for a trip
cards.body: When packing a suitcase, you can't bring everything. If you try to shrink the size of every item, you still end up with too many small items. Sometimes, you just need to leave things behind.

cards.title: What is Lasso?
cards.body: Lasso Regression (L1 regularization) is a technique that penalizes the sum of the absolute values of the model's weights, forcing less important feature weights to become exactly zero.

cards.title: The absolute threshold
cards.body: Think of Ridge as a dimmer switch that turns down the volume of all features but keeps them playing. Lasso is like an on/off switch: it completely turns off the features that aren't contributing enough.

cards.title: Predicting house prices
cards.body: If you have 100 features (like wall color, mailbox shape, and square footage), Lasso will set the weights of unimportant features (like mailbox shape) to exactly zero, leaving only the essential features.

cards.title: Not just smaller weights
cards.body: A common myth is that Lasso just makes weights smaller like Ridge does. In reality, Lasso's mathematical penalty shape has sharp corners that drive weights all the way to zero, actually removing features.

cards.title: Ridge vs Lasso
cards.body: How L1 and L2 penalties behave:

left.text:
Penalizes squared weights.
Shrinks weights close to zero.
Keeps all features.

right.text:
Penalizes absolute weights.
Shrinks weights to exactly zero.
Discards useless features.

exercises.prompt: What penalty does Lasso Regression add to the cost function?

options.text: The sum of the absolute values of the weights
The sum of the squared values of the weights
The product of the weights and the features

exercises.explain: Lasso adds an L1 penalty, which is the sum of the absolute values of the weights.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Lasso regression can shrink some weights to exactly zero.
exercises.explain: True! Lasso's penalty can reduce less important weights to exactly zero, effectively removing those features from the model.

exercises.sentence: While Ridge regression shrinks weights close to zero, Lasso regression can set them to ___ zero.
exercises.explain: Lasso has the unique property of driving weights to exactly zero.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes the cost penalty used by Lasso Regression?
exercises.explain: Lasso uses L1 regularization, which sums the absolute values of the weights.

exercises.prompt: You are building a model to predict customer churn with 200 features, but your server has very limited memory. You want a model that performs well but ignores useless features entirely. Which regularization should you use?

options.text: Lasso Regression, because it pushes weights of unimportant features to exactly zero, giving a simpler model.
Ridge Regression, because it shrinks all weights evenly and keeps all 200 features.
No regularization, because memory limits do not affect model execution.

exercises.explain: Perfect! By forcing useless weights to zero, Lasso performs automatic feature selection, producing a sparse model that uses less memory. Next: let's look closer at how Lasso acts as a feature selector!
